skill.name: histolab
skill.description: Guides digital pathology workflows with histolab for loading whole slide images, creating tissue masks, extracting tiles, applying preprocessing filters, and visualizing quality checks.
skill.summary: Process whole slide pathology images with histolab tile extraction, masks, filters, and visualization workflows.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found the flagged external command, dynamic import, weak crypto, and reconnaissance matches are false positives from Markdown code fences, inline Python examples, and digital pathology terminology. No prompt injection, credential access, network exfiltration, or malicious execution intent was found; the remaining risk is low because examples read and write local slide, thumbnail, and tile files.

low_findings.title: Local Filesystem Processing Guidance
low_findings.description: The skill legitimately guides users to load WSI files, save thumbnails, read extracted tile images, create output directories, and iterate local slide folders. This is expected for histolab workflows, but users should apply it only to trusted paths and controlled datasets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem behavior is clearly documented and domain-appropriate, with no evidence of destructive paths or secret harvesting. Confidence is high enough to record a low risk because local file I/O is central to the workflow.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The numerous Ruby backtick and external command findings map to Markdown fences, inline code formatting, and normal Python examples. The reviewed locations do not instruct shell backtick execution or command injection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Representative flagged lines show fenced bash or Python documentation, not executable skill code. The same pattern repeats across the static finding list, so the static external command category is dismissed.

low_findings.title: Static Dynamic Import Findings Are Python Import False Positives
low_findings.description: The dynamic import findings occur on multi-line Python import examples for histolab filters. They are not dynamic module loading, eval, or runtime code generation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact lines contain static import statements in Markdown examples. There is no import expression that loads attacker-controlled module names.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Crypto And Reconnaissance Findings Are Domain False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and reconnaissance findings are triggered by benign terms such as random seeds for reproducibility, HED stain decomposition, slide dimensions, levels, and local path iteration. No cryptographic API, system inventory command, or host reconnaissance intent was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed flagged areas show reproducible sampling parameters and pathology image metadata, not security-sensitive cryptography or machine reconnaissance. No supporting malicious context was found in the broader search.

content.value_statement: Whole slide pathology images are large and difficult to prepare for analysis. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical histolab workflows for masks, tiles, filters, and visual checks.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to load and inspect whole slide images with the histolab Slide class.
Guides RandomTiler, GridTiler, and ScoreTiler workflows for extracting image tiles.
Describes TissueMask, BiggestTissueBoxMask, and custom BinaryMask usage for tissue regions.
Documents preprocessing filters for thresholding, morphology, stain channels, and artifact reduction.
Shows visualization patterns for thumbnails, masks, tile previews, and extraction review.
limitations: Does not include executable scripts, notebooks, or automated pipeline runners.
Requires a working Python environment with histolab and imaging dependencies installed.
Does not diagnose disease or replace pathologist review.
Large WSI processing still depends on local storage, memory, and OpenSlide support.

use_cases.title: Prepare Training Tiles
use_cases.description: Extract reproducible pathology image tiles from WSI files for model training and dataset curation.

use_cases.title: Validate Tissue Masks
use_cases.description: Compare tissue masks and preprocessing filters before running large extraction jobs.

use_cases.title: Review Slide Quality
use_cases.description: Generate thumbnails, mask overlays, and tile grids to inspect staining, artifacts, and coverage.

prompt_templates.title: Load a Slide
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for one WSI file
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me the basic histolab steps to load one SVS slide, inspect its dimensions, and save a thumbnail.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Random Tiles
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a small exploratory tile set
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me configure RandomTiler for 100 tissue-rich tiles from a whole slide image with reproducible sampling.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Mask Strategies
prompt_templates.scenario: Choose a tissue mask for unusual slide layouts
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare TissueMask, BiggestTissueBoxMask, and a custom BinaryMask for slides with artifacts and multiple tissue sections.

prompt_templates.title: Design a WSI Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a histolab workflow for multi-slide tile extraction, preprocessing, quality control, and metadata reporting.

output_examples.input: User asks how to sample tiles from a prostate whole slide image.
output_examples.output: The skill outlines slide loading, RandomTiler settings, tissue filtering thresholds, preview steps, and extraction review checks.

output_examples.input: User asks how to remove background and pen artifacts before extraction.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends tissue masks, color-space filters, morphology filters, and visual validation before batch processing.

output_examples.input: User asks how to inspect extracted tiles for quality.
output_examples.output: The skill describes thumbnail review, tile grids, score reports, tissue coverage checks, and manual review points.

best_practices: Preview masks and tile locations before running full extraction jobs.
Use fixed random seeds when comparing experiments or building reproducible datasets.
Start with small tile counts and lower pyramid levels before scaling to full-resolution processing.
anti_patterns: Do not process level 0 whole slide regions without checking memory and output size.
Do not trust automatic tissue masks without visual review on representative slides.
Do not use extracted tiles for clinical decisions without validated medical workflows.

faq.question: What image formats does this skill discuss?
faq.answer: It focuses on WSI formats commonly handled by histolab, including SVS, TIFF, and NDPI.

faq.question: Can it extract tiles for machine learning datasets?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents random, grid, and score-based tile extraction strategies for dataset preparation.

faq.question: Does it run histolab automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. The user must run histolab in their own Python environment.

faq.question: Does it include medical diagnosis guidance?
faq.answer: No. It supports image processing workflows and does not provide diagnostic conclusions.

faq.question: How should users handle large slide files?
faq.answer: They should preview thumbnails, choose suitable pyramid levels, and test small batches before full extraction.

faq.question: Why are masks important?
faq.answer: Masks help exclude background, artifacts, and non-tissue areas before tile extraction.
